titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Arthur G. Childs papers AD.MC.099
titlestmt.author: Brad Bauer

publicationstmt.publisher: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

address.addressline: New York University Abu Dhabi, C-2

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-05-04 15:47:12 +0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

repository.corpname: NYU Abu Dhabi, Archives and Special Collections

did.unittitle: Arthur G. Child papers

physdesc.extent: in four document boxes and one flat box.

did.abstract: Diaries, photographs, books, pamphlets, and realia, documenting the military and civilian career of Arthur G. Child, an Anglican priest who served in parishes in England, chiefly in and around Chester, between 1890 and 1935, and as a chaplain with the British Army in the Middle East during World War I.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions governing access
accessrestrict.p: The Arthur G. Child papers are open and available for use in the Archives and Special Collections department of the New York University Abu Dhabi Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Arthur G. Child papers are arranged in four series: I. Diaries, II. Photographs, III. Publications and printed materials, IV. Various: biographical, ephemera, and realia. Each series in arranged in roughly chronological order, with the "Photographs" series also grouped by topic and type of image.

bioghist.head: Biographical note
bioghist.p: Rev. Arthur Gascoigne Child (1867-1966) was an Anglican priest and served as a chaplain in the British Army during World War I. Child was born in 1867 in North Nibley, Gloucestershire, England, the son of Thomas H.I Child (1834-1926), a clergyman in the Church of England, and Florence (née Crossman, 1836-1910) Child. He had five siblings, Ethel (1866-1880), Gertrude (1868-1951), Harold (1869-1945), Ella (b. 1872) and Agnes Maud (1873-1953). He graduated from Keble College, Oxford, with a degree in theology in 1888, and then served as a curate at St. John the Baptist Church in Chester from 1889-1893, after which he served as a vicar at St. James, in Sutton, Macclesfield from 1899-1904, and then was rector at Middlewich, in Chester, from 1904 until the outbreak of World War I.

At that time, Child enlisted in the British Army as a chaplain, joining the 1/7th Cheshire Regiment, which became part of 53rd (Welsh) Division. Child saw action at Gallipoli, where he arrived in August 1915 and was wounded in December of that year, the First and Second Battles of Gaza, and following that, he served as chaplain in charge of the Suez Canal Zone from December 1917 to August 1918.

Following the war, Child returned to his position as rector in Middlewich, where he served until 1921, and then was vicar at St. John's in Chester from 1925-1936, at which point he retired from parish ministry. From 1937-1939, he served as a chaplain at House of Mercy, a residential school for girls, in Rothwell, West Yorkshire, but then moved to Somerset, to be nearer to friends. Rev. Child died in Taunton, on 27 September 1966.
[Source: baptism, census, and death records in Ancestry.com, and description from vendor's catalog]

prefercite.head: Preferred citation
prefercite.p: [item], [folder title], [box/folder number], Arthur G. Child papers, MC-099, New York University Abu Dhabi Library, Abu Dhabi, UAE.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Arthur G. Child papers consist of diaries, photographs, books, pamphlets, and realia, documenting the military and civilian career of Child, an Anglican priest who served in parishes in England, and as a chaplain with the British Army in the Middle East during World War I.

The "Diaries" series consists largely of pocket calendars titled "The Clerical Diary and Visiting List," which were used by clergy to note appointments and meetings with congregants, church events, and other matters of congregational life. These cover selected years from 1906 to 1925, during the period when Child served as rector of the parish church in Middlewich, Chester. The one notable exception in this series is the diary that he kept in 1917, which is contained in a small volume titled "Lett's Diary, 1916," dating from January to September of 1917, and in which he noted events, meetings, journeys, and burials at which he officiated while serving as chaplain with the 1/7 Chester Regiment of the 53rd (Welsh) Division, based in el-Arish, in the northern Sinai Peninsula, during this period.

The "Photographs" series contains images of his family, dating back to his childhood in the 1870s, as well as a few portraits from his university years at Oxford, and later images with family members and parishioners, chiefly from the 1930s to the 1950s and in his retirement, during the 1950s. While there are a signficant number of images from the period of his military service in World War I, none of them are identified, and they are grouped together in likely categories, such as images from training in Britain around 1914, images of individual and groups of unidentified soldiers, scenery in the Middle East (likely in or near Suez, the Sinai Peninsula, and Gaza), as well as of visits made to ancient ruins in Egypt, near Luxor. The remaining images are unidentified, but likely of friends, family, and parishioners in the churches that he served. Also included are postcards and photographs of churches around Britain. Larger format photographs from all periods of his life are kept in the oversize box (Box 5) that also contains realia

The series "Publications and Printed Material" contains a variety of materials, most of which either were related to his religious beliefs and ministry, but also in relation to the history of Chester and the surrounding region. Notable titles include the Greek New Testament that Child's father had used, and judging from the notes and annotations, which Child may have used as well, and also a printed copy of an oratorio by Haydn, which had belonged to Child's mother. The majority of the publications consist of pamphlets, treating various religious themes, including several by F.S.M. Bennett, the dean of Chester Cathedral, as well as a printed program for the installation of Cosmo Gordon Lang as Archbishop of Canterbury in 1928, and a pamphlet titled "The Moral Issues of War," by W.R. Matthews, the dean of St. Paul's Cathedral in London, from 1940. There are also pamphlets of a literary and historic nature, such as multiple printings of poetry from E.K. Ellis, a friend of Child's and multiple times a recipient of the Seatonian Poetry Prize at Cambridge, and printed speeches given at Founder's Day at St. Deiniol's Library, the library founded by former Prime Minister W.E. Gladstone, in nearby Hawarden, Wales.

The last series, "Various: biographical, ephemera, and realia," includes items from various aspects of Child's life, including a card he gave his father as a child, a class directory from Keble College, Oxford (1888), as well as an embroidered tapestry with the college's emblem, scattered correspondence to and from Child, his obituary, his clerical stole, with military insignia; and a wooden shield with the insignia of the Cheshire Regiment, among other items.

userestrict.head: Conditions governing use
userestrict.p: Unpublished as well as published content within the collection may be protected by copyright, and any uses of such material beyond what is commonly accepted as "fair use" require the express written permission of the copyright holder.

did.unittitle: Diaries
1917 (in 1916 Lett's Diary)
Photographs
Child, Arthur G., and family
Child, Arthur G., portraits
Child, Arthur G., portrait
Child, Arthur G., with friends and others
Child family, portraits
Child family, portraits
Crossman, Florence (mother)
Military: landscapes in Middle East, unidentified
Military: portraits of individuals, unidentified
Military: portraits of groups of soldiers, in Britain and Middle East
Military: training in Britain, positive prints and negatives in original sleeves
Military: Middle East, various, including one scene in Khan Yunis, Gaza
Photographs, scenes of ancient Egyptian ruins (1): near Luxor, Ramesseum, including Colossi of Memnon and Fallen Colossus of Ozymandias, with soldiers posing in front
Photographs, scenes of ancient Egyptian ruins (2): Karnak Temple complex, Luxor
Photographs, unidentified, church altars
Photographs, unidentified: churches
Photographs, unidentified: clergy
Photographs, unidentified: family and friends
Photographs, unidentified: portraits
Photographs, unidentified: theatrical performances
Photographs, unidentified: wedding parties and other gatherings
Postcards: Keble College, Oxford
Postcards: Holy Island
Postcards: St. John's Church, Chester
Postcards: Shrine of Our Lady, Walsingham
Postcards: churches, various
Publications and printed material
"Haydn's Oratorio : The Creation," arranged by Vincent Novello, 143 p., (London: J. Alfred Novello), with ownership mark of Florence Crossman
Greek New Testament, belonging to Child's father, Rev. T.H.I. Child, 184 pages, with handwritten notes and annotations (Leipzig : Brockhaus)
"Chester Diocesan Gazette : a monthly record of church work in the diocese," volumes 9 and 10 (in one bound volume)
Moberly, R.C. "Considerations upon Disestablishment and Disendowment," 73 pages (Oxford : James Parker)
Scott, S. Cooper. "Guide to the Church of Saint John the Baptist, Chester," 48 pages (Chester : Phillipson and Golder)
"Form of Consecration of a Church Burial Ground in the Diocese of Chester," 20 pages (Chester : Phillipson and Golder)
Viscount Halifax, "Catholic Unity and the Relation of National Churches to the Church Universal," 38 pages (London : English Church Union)
Marson, Charles L., "Huppim and Muppim. A few words upon the sore need of Religious Education," 16 p. (Oxford, London : A.R. Mowbray)
Taunt, Henry W. "Fairford Church, Lechlade, and the District," 84 pages (Fairford : Thomas Powell)
"Winchester Cathedral, a short history for visitors," Sixth Edition, 16 pages (Winchester : Warren and Son)
"Middlewich : Official Guide," 18 pages (London : Vickery, Kyrle and Co., Ltd.)
Bennett, F.S.M. "The Nature of a Cathedral," 78 pages (Chester : Phillipson and Golder)
Bennett, F.S.M. "An Open Letter to the Chairman and Council of the C.E.M.S.," 40 pages, with author inscription (Chester : Phillipson and Golder)
Bennett, F.S.M. "Mary Jane and Harry John, or Home the Premier School," 32 pages (London, Oxford : A.R. Mowbray)
Bennett, F.S.M. "On Cathedrals in the Meantime," 43 pages (London : The Faith Press, Ltd.)
"Order of Service for the Enthronement of the Most Reverend Father in God Cosmo, Lord Archbishop of Canterbury, Primate of All England," Canterbury, 23 pages (printed at the University Press, Oxford)
Webb, Albert. "Glastonbury Abbey : a hasty story of the Abbey and a descriptive guide to the Abbey ruins," 37 pages (Glastonbury : Avalon Press)
Bennett, F.S.M. "The Resurrection of the Organism," 40 pages (Chester : Phillilpson and Golder)
Lawrence, C.F. "Glimpses of Bygone Middlewich," "Middlewich Doings in Olden Days," "The Story of Bygone Middlewich" [three separate booklets]
Forrest, S.J. "The Church in Reconstruction," with illustrations by E.W. Forrest, 31 pages (Hove : Coelian Press)
Hole, Rev. Donald. "The Church and the Strolling Player," 18 pages (London : Actors' Church Union)
Ellis, E.K. Three booklets containing the texts of the Seatonian Prize Poems, "God and the Mind of Man," "St. Etheldreda," and "The Call of Abraham" (Cambridge : University Press)
Ellis, E.K. "St. Patrick and other poems," 53 pages, with inscription from author on flyleaf, and enclosed letter by Ellis to Child, to accompany the book (Cambridge : W. Heffer and Sons)
"The Handbook to Chester," 48 pages (Chester : Phillipson and Golder)
St. Deiniol's Library, Founder's Day addresses : "Mr. Gladstone," "Some Memories of Gladstone," and "Memories of a Sacred and Inspiring Friendship"
"Sermon preached by the Bishop of Monmouth in S. Deiniol's Church, Hawarden on Founder's Day, June 29th, 1934 [4 pages] (Hawarden : Caxton Press)
Downton, Francis Murray. "Clerical Celibacy, an essay addressed to the clergy of the Anglican Communion," 46 pages (London : Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge)
Matthews, W.R. "The Moral Issues of War," 41 pages (London : Eyre and Spottiswoode)
Duffield, A.W.G. "'But as Yesterday,' a short history of the church of St. John the Baptist in the City of Chester," 20 pages (Chester : W.H. Evans Sons)
Various: biographical, ephemera, and realia
Biographical: Child, T.H.I., estate settlement documents
Biographical: correspondence
Biographical: House of Mercy, Horbury; annual report
Biographical: Middlemas Term, Keble College, Oxford [class directory]
Biographical: obituaries of Arthur G. Child
Ephemera, various
Ephemera, various
Ephemera, various
Realia: Clerical stole, black, embroidered with emblem of CTF and metal riband attached, displaying medals of 1914-1915 (British War Medal, Star, Victory Medal, and oak leaf cluster)
Realia: Embroidered arms of Keble College, Oxford
Realia: Shield, wooden, with painted badge of the Cheshire Regiment
Realia: "Poem, A Vicarage," by Drusilla Child, dedicated to Arthur Gascoigne Child, printed on card stock, 25 x 33 cm.
Realia: photogravure of Major-General Stanley F. Mott, C.O. 53rd Welsh Division, after an oil painting and signed by Mott, 23 x 16 cm., matted
